created_by: Wilderculture WILD Toolbox session (Claude) — priority-gap batch. NO Wilderculture field sheet exists for this habitat: the record is built from agency/scientific sources (JNCC BAP, NVC MG3, NE/NatureScot), and the WILD-overlay fields are Claude-derived PROVISIONAL (see overlay_provenance), to be reviewed or replaced by a practitioner sheet. v3.3.

overlay_provenance.note: Unlike the 11 sheet-backed records, there is no source_level-7 practitioner field sheet for g3b. The ukhab_identity / ecology / cross_references / forage / management blocks rest on agency and scientific sources (high confidence). The wild_assessment block (elemental dials, zone, value axes) is Claude's reasoned interpretation by analogy to the sheet-backed records — flagged provisional, moderate confidence — and should be reviewed and, ideally, replaced by a Wilderculture field sheet before operational use.

ukhab_level5_states.g3b5: Mountain hay meadows (Annex I H6520) — the NVC MG3 priority type

ukhab_identity.ukhab_level5_note: Keyed at g3b. g3b5 (Mountain hay meadows, H6520) is the Annex I type recorded per parcel where the MG3 community qualifies.
ukhab_identity.composite_pk_note: Single Level-4 type; PK = g3b. Sibling of g3a (lowland meadows, H6510) and g3c (other neutral grassland). Verified: UKHab master data sheet, Land App BNG list, applied BNG registers.
ukhab_identity.annex1_habitat: H6520 Mountain hay meadows (the g3b5 / MG3 type)

sources.title: UK Habitat Classification v2.01 (master data sheet + BNG list)
sources.specific_claim: g3 = Neutral grassland; g3b = Upland hay meadows; g3b5 = Mountain hay meadows (H6520). g3a = lowland meadows (H6510), g3c = other neutral grassland.

nvc_communities: MG3 Anthoxanthum odoratum–Geranium sylvaticum grassland (the upland hay meadow)
transitions to MG3b / MG8 / MG6 on wetter or improved ground
cross_references.nvc_communities_notes: MG3 is the defining community — the most species-rich British mesotrophic grassland, effectively confined to traditionally-managed upland hay meadows.
cross_references.phase1_equivalent: B2.1 Neutral grassland — unimproved (upland hay meadow)
cross_references.wild_sheet_ref_note: No Wilderculture field sheet exists (priority-habitat gap). See overlay_provenance.

sources.title: UK BAP Priority Habitat Description — Upland Hay Meadows
sources.specific_claim: Upland hay meadows = NVC MG3, Annex I H6520; restricted, highly threatened; traditionally managed by late hay cut + aftermath grazing + low inputs.

ecology.description: The most species-rich mesotrophic grassland in Britain and one of its rarest — traditionally-managed upland hay meadows (NVC MG3) on neutral soils (pH ~5.5–6.5) above ~300 m, effectively confined to the northern Pennine dales (Teesdale, Weardale, Swaledale, Wensleydale) with small amounts in Scotland and Wales. A semi-natural priority habitat held in being by a centuries-old management cycle: a single LATE hay cut, aftermath and light spring grazing, and very low inputs. Estimated to cover under ~1,000 ha in England and to have declined dramatically (much of the resource lost to agricultural improvement).
condition_indicators_good: The MG3 indicator assemblage present and frequent — wood crane's-bill, globeflower, lady's-mantles, melancholy thistle, great burnet, pignut, yellow-rattle
A diverse, herb-rich sward with no rye-grass dominance; flowering and seed-set achieved before the cut
Low fertility maintained — no inorganic fertiliser, slurry or reseeding
Traditional cycle intact: late hay cut, aftermath grazing, light spring grazing
condition_indicators_poor: Agricultural improvement — inorganic fertiliser/slurry, early silage cutting, reseeding (the dominant cause of loss; collapses MG3 to g4)
Loss of indicator herbs; rye-grass/white-clover or rank grass dominance
ecosystem_functions: An exceptionally diverse plant community and the invertebrate/pollinator resource it supports
A genetic and cultural reservoir of traditional hay-meadow flora (incl. rare lady's-mantles)
Hay and aftermath grazing within a low-input farming system
Pollinator forage and seed source for meadow restoration elsewhere
ecology.whole_system_role: A farmed priority habitat — the high point of the traditionally-managed neutral-grassland gradient, sustained by management rather than abandonment, and a seed source for wider meadow restoration.

successional_neighbours.habitat_code: g4 modified grassland / g3c other neutral
successional_neighbours.note: Fertiliser/silage/reseeding collapses MG3 to g4; abandonment drifts to rank g3c then scrub.
Sits within the dale mosaic alongside upland calcareous and acid grassland and flushes.

sources.title: Natural England / Hay Time — upland hay meadow restoration and management
sources.author_org: Natural England / Yorkshire Dales Millennium Trust
sources.specific_claim: MG3 distribution, decline, and the late-cut/aftermath-grazing/low-input management; green-hay restoration practice.

ukhab_condition.condition_grade_note: Assigned per parcel; the MG3 indicator assemblage and low fertility are the diagnostics.

condition_grade_descriptors.label: Favourable
condition_grade_descriptors.description: Full MG3 indicator assemblage, herb-rich, no rye-grass dominance; traditional late-cut + aftermath-grazing cycle; low fertility; seed-set achieved.

condition_grade_descriptors.label: Unfavourable recovering
condition_grade_descriptors.description: Indicators returning under reinstated traditional management / green-hay restoration.

condition_grade_descriptors.label: Unfavourable no change
condition_grade_descriptors.description: Some indicator loss; slightly enriched or cut a little early; recoverable.

condition_grade_descriptors.label: Unfavourable declining
condition_grade_descriptors.description: Improvement (fertiliser/silage/reseed) eroding MG3, OR abandonment to rank grass/scrub.

condition_grade_descriptors.label: Destroyed / converted
condition_grade_descriptors.description: Reseeded/fertilised to g4 or otherwise lost — MG3 gone.

grade_field_indicators: MG3 indicator herbs (wood crane's-bill, globeflower, lady's-mantles, etc.)

sources.title: UK BAP — Upland Hay Meadows
sources.specific_claim: MG3 indicator assemblage and low inputs define favourable condition; improvement is the principal threat.

wild_assessment.overlay_status: PROVISIONAL — Claude-derived (no practitioner field sheet); review/replace before operational use.

abiotic_template.parent_material_geology: Neutral mesotrophic soils (pH ~5.5–6.5) on dale floors and lower slopes
abiotic_template.climate: Upland, cool and moist; short growing season
abiotic_template.altitude_range_m: ~300 m and above (the upland/lowland split from g3a)
abiotic_template.aspect_exposure: Dale floors and sheltered lower slopes; often valley-bottom meadows
abiotic_template.bioclimate_notes: Northern Pennine dales above all (Teesdale, Weardale, Swaledale, Wensleydale); small amounts in Scotland/Wales.

elemental_conditions.convention_note: CURRENT-STATE reading for a semi-natural priority habitat (NOT a Regenerate production-sward target). PROVISIONAL — Claude-derived, no field sheet.

water.state: Free-draining to seasonally moist neutral soils
water.descriptor: Dale-floor meadows; moist but not waterlogged (wet ground tends toward rush/MG8).

earth.pedogenic_process: neutral mesotrophic cycling at LOW fertility
earth.descriptor: Moderately fertile but deliberately kept low-nutrient by removing the hay crop and minimal manure only — low fertility is what sustains the diversity.

air.descriptor: Aerobic neutral soils; a productive but unforced sward.

fire.npp_level: Moderate (a real hay crop, far below improved swards)
fire.descriptor: A genuinely productive meadow, but the productivity is modest and channelled into a diverse hay crop, not maximised — high diversity per unit production.

successional_envelope.current_stage: Plagioclimax — held by the traditional hay-cut + grazing cycle
successional_envelope.successional_pathway: Maintained by management: late hay cut + aftermath grazing + low inputs holds MG3. Improve it (fertiliser/silage/reseed) → collapses to g4; abandon it → rank grass → scrub. Like calcareous grassland, it is a managed equilibrium, not a free-running succession.

wild_assessment.energy_flow_role: A high-diversity, modest-production farmed meadow — diversity and a hay crop coexisting under low-input management; a seed source for wider restoration.
wild_assessment.wild_zone_rationale: PROVISIONAL. A rare, Annex I, semi-natural priority habitat → Protect & Restore: maintain the traditional management of surviving meadows (the resource is too rare and too easily lost to do otherwise). Restoration of species-poor/improved meadows toward MG3 (green-hay strewing, reduced inputs, correct cut timing) is the Regenerate action. Note the interface: this is a FARMED priority habitat, so it sits where Protect & Restore meets working agriculture — distinct from the Regenerate production swards (c1b/g4), which are improved leys, not priority habitat.
wild_assessment.disturbance_regime: A precise management cycle, not a stocking rate: ONE late hay cut (allow flowering/seed-set, traditionally not before mid-July), aftermath grazing (cattle especially) to remove regrowth and create gaps, light spring grazing then shut up for hay; very low farmyard-manure inputs only.

wild_management_target.detail: Maintain (or restore) the MG3 community by keeping the traditional cycle: late hay cut after seed-set, aftermath + light spring grazing, NO inorganic fertiliser/slurry/silage/reseeding/herbicides. For restoration, reduce fertility, correct cut timing, and add seed via green hay from a good donor meadow. Re-grade trigger applies.
integrity_indicators: MG3 indicator herbs present and flowering

wild_assessment.ecological_value_notes: Among the highest — the most species-rich British mesotrophic grassland, an Annex I priority habitat reduced to a tiny, fragmented resource largely in the northern Pennine dales; effectively irreplaceable once improved.

rarity_irreplaceability.notes: One of the rarest grasslands in the UK (under ~1,000 ha in England); MG3 cannot be quickly recreated once lost to improvement.

diversity.notes: The most species-rich mesotrophic grassland in Britain, incl. rare lady's-mantles.

energy_flow_contribution.notes: Modest production (a hay crop), but exceptional diversity and a pollinator/seed resource per unit area.

wild_assessment.naturalistic_lu_ha_note: Not a 'remove stock' habitat — it requires its management cycle. Operative grazing is aftermath + light spring grazing at a conservation level (see conservation_lu_ha); the hay cut is the main offtake.
wild_assessment.mosaic_role_note: The herb-rich hay meadow of the dale floor; a pollinator and seed-source hub within the dale mosaic.
wild_assessment.preferential_grazing_magnet_note: Not relevant in the open-hill sense — this is an enclosed, cut-and-aftermath-grazed meadow, not free-range hill grazing. The risk is mistimed/over-intensive grazing or cutting, not a grazing-magnet effect.
wild_assessment.process_indicators_note: PROVISIONAL overlay. The diagnostic is the MG3 assemblage sustained by the management cycle.

sources.title: UK BAP — Upland Hay Meadows
sources.specific_claim: Traditional late-cut/aftermath-grazing/low-input management sustains MG3; improvement destroys it.

sources.title: Claude-derived WILD overlay (no practitioner field sheet)
sources.specific_claim: Elemental dials, zone placement and value axes inferred by analogy to the sheet-backed records — PROVISIONAL, for review/replacement.

forage.years_unimproved: Traditionally unimproved (decades to centuries) — improvement destroys the habitat
forage.overall_palatability_notes: A genuine hay crop plus nutritious aftermath grazing — palatable and productive, but the conservation point is to take a MODEST crop late and graze the aftermath, not to maximise yield. Treated under the semi-natural convention (offtake via the hay cut + light grazing), NOT as a Regenerate production sward.

forage_yield_by_grade.notes: Indicative LOW-INPUT hay yield (roughly 1.5–3.5 t DM/ha) — well below improved meadows; figure is literature-class and PROVISIONAL, verify against scheme/site data. Plus aftermath grazing.

forage_yield_literature.notes: Low-input upland hay meadow yields are modest and variable; quoted ranges vary widely. Treat as indicative only.

sources.title: NE / Hay Time upland hay meadow guidance
sources.specific_claim: Upland hay meadows are low-yielding low-input systems relative to improved grassland.

forage.utilisation_factor_note: Semi-natural convention — not modelled as gross DM × utilisation. Offtake is the hay cut plus conservation aftermath/spring grazing.

conservation_lu_ha.value: Aftermath + light spring grazing at a conservation level — scheme-tied and site-specific; commonly cattle aftermath grazing in late summer/autumn and light spring grazing before shutting up for hay. No single year-round stocking figure (it is a cut meadow, not a grazed pasture).
conservation_lu_ha.scheme: GLU (England — CS/SFI meadow options) / LU (Scotland — AECS) — scheme-tied; verify current option prescriptions
conservation_lu_ha.basis_note: Grazing is the aftermath/spring component of a hay-meadow cycle, not continuous pasture stocking. Cattle aftermath grazing is favoured (tread, gap creation, no tight sheep grazing of the sward). Agri-environment options set the detail. Verified June 2026 — re-check scheme options before contracts.

sources.title: UK BAP — Upland Hay Meadows
sources.specific_claim: Aftermath and light spring grazing within the hay cycle; cattle favoured.

sources.title: England CS/SFI and Scotland AECS species-rich/hay-meadow options
sources.specific_claim: Hay-meadow options prescribe cut dates, aftermath grazing and input limits; details change between scheme rounds.

forage.palatability_rank: High (hay + aftermath) — but managed for diversity, not yield
forage.nutritional_quality_note: Diverse herb-rich hay is good-quality traditional fodder; the meadow is kept at low fertility deliberately. No toxicity theme.

seasonal_forage_curve.spring: Light spring grazing, then shut up for hay
seasonal_forage_curve.summer: Grown for hay; LATE cut after seed-set (traditionally mid-July onward)

optimal_grazing_animals.notes: Cattle aftermath grazing creates gaps and treads seed in without tight-grazing the sward; native/traditional breeds suit the low-input system.

unsuitable_animals.breed: Heavy/continuous sheep grazing of the meadow
unsuitable_animals.reason: Tight continuous sheep grazing selects against the tall herbs and erodes MG3; sheep better used lightly and seasonally only.

timing_windows.preferred_season: Light spring graze → shut up → LATE hay cut (after seed-set) → aftermath graze
timing_windows.rationale: Allow flowering and seed-set before cutting; aftermath grazing maintains structure and low fertility
timing_windows.avoid: Early/silage cutting; fertiliser/slurry; reseeding; herbicides; heavy continuous grazing
timing_windows.per_species_notes: Cattle for aftermath; sheep only light/seasonal.

management.intensity_guidance: Maintain the traditional low-input cycle precisely (see timing). For restoration: reduce fertility, correct cut timing, add seed via green hay from a good local donor meadow.
management.regulatory_note: Many upland hay meadows are SSSI/SAC (H6520) and depend on agri-environment funding (England CS/SFI; Scotland AECS; Wales) — option prescriptions set cut dates, aftermath grazing and input limits and change between scheme rounds; verify current options and any designated-site consents before changing management. NO field sheet exists — confirm practitioner detail with Wilderculture before operational use.
management.monitoring_protocol: MG3 indicator assemblage, rye-grass/fertility signs, cut timing and seed-set, aftermath grazing structure, scrub/rank-grass encroachment. Apply the re-grade trigger.

bng_unit_value.value_note: Very high distinctiveness — priority habitat (H6520 / MG3). Among the highest BNG values; effectively irreplaceable once improved.

sources.title: Statutory Biodiversity Metric
sources.specific_claim: Upland hay meadows very high distinctiveness; confirm exact label.

key_constants: Geranium sylvaticum (wood crane's-bill — the MG3 signature)
key_positive_indicators: wood crane's-bill, globeflower (Trollius europaeus), melancholy thistle (Cirsium heterophyllum)
lady's-mantles (incl. rare Alchemilla), pignut, great burnet, sweet vetch (Hippocrepis/Vicia), yellow-rattle (Rhinanthus minor)
a herb-rich sward with flowering and seed-set; no rye-grass dominance
key_negative_indicators: loss of indicator herbs; scrub encroachment
integrity_indicators_fauna: meadow pollinators (bumblebees, hoverflies) and the broader meadow invertebrate community
seed-eating/farmland birds using the meadow and aftermath
integrity_indicators_flora: the MG3 indicator assemblage itself is the headline integrity indicator — wood crane's-bill + globeflower + lady's-mantles + melancholy thistle signal intact upland hay meadow
target_species_composition: full MG3 assemblage with the signature herbs flowering
species_list.note: Skeleton only — full records and the rare-lady's-mantle/pollinator joins in Phase 2B. NB built WITHOUT a practitioner field sheet.

fields_fully_populated: ecology / condition / management from high-confidence agency sources
the traditional management cycle and improvement-as-threat
fields_partially_populated: wild_assessment — PROVISIONAL, Claude-derived (no field sheet; moderate confidence)
conservation grazing — scheme-tied, verify current options
fields_pending_research: A Wilderculture field sheet for g3b (the proper source of the WILD overlay)
Site MG3 condition and rare-Alchemilla records (Phase 2B)
data_completeness.notes: FIRST record built without a practitioner field sheet — the flagged priority gap. g3b is among the rarest grasslands in the UK (MG3, Annex I H6520), a FARMED priority habitat sustained by a precise low-input cycle (late hay cut + aftermath grazing) and destroyed by improvement. Protect & Restore, sitting where conservation meets working agriculture — and distinct from the Regenerate production swards (c1b/g4). The WILD overlay is provisional pending a Wilderculture sheet.
